rule: RFC_READ_TABLE has a 512-byte line buffer limit. When total field widths exceed this, SAP returns TABLE_WITHOUT_DATA or DATA_BUFFER_EXCEEDED.
why: Fixed MAX_FIELDS_PER_CALL=8 works for most tables but fails on ESSR (which has very wide session description fields). Even 5 fields overflow the buffer.
how_to_apply:
`rfc_helpers.py` now auto-detects the right chunk size:
1. Try 8 fields -> if TABLE_WITHOUT_DATA, halve to 4
2. Try 4 fields -> if still fails, halve to 2
3. If even 2 fields fail -> genuinely empty or auth issue

This is in `rfc_read_paginated()` in the split-field section. The adaptive logic runs once per table per WHERE clause, then uses that chunk size for all pages.

Additional learnings:
- ESSR max fields = 4 (LOESSION field is ~200 bytes alone)
- BSIS/BSAS max fields = 8 (works fine with 28 fields split into 4 chunks)
- EKPO max fields = 8 (19 fields, 3 chunks)
- BKPF max fields = ALL (12 fields fit in one call, no split needed)

rule: VPN drops are normal during long SAP extractions at UNESCO. Without auto-reconnect, each drop kills the extraction thread and remaining months get 0-row checkpoints (which block retries).
why: BSIS extraction (2.29M rows, ~2 hours) required 4+ restarts due to VPN drops. Each restart was manual and lost time.
how_to_apply:
- `get_connection("P01")` returns a `ConnectionGuard` (not raw pyrfc.Connection)
- ConnectionGuard.call() catches connection-closed/timeout/CPIC errors
- Retries up to 3 times with escalating wait (10s, 20s, 30s)
- Transparent to callers -- rfc_read_paginated works unchanged

Error substrings that trigger reconnect:
- "connection closed", "partner not reached", "timeout"
- "communication failure", "CPIC_", "RFC_COMMUNICATION_FAILURE"
- "RFC_INVALID_HANDLE", "connection has been closed"

Pair with error-safe checkpoints: if a period errors with 0 rows, do NOT write checkpoint file. This allows the period to be retried on next run.

rule: User wants AUTONOMOUS execution. Stop asking for permission or confirmation before running bash commands.
why: Every session the user says "execute autonomously" and then Claude still asks "can I run this?" or "should I proceed?". This wastes time and frustrates the user.
how_to_apply:
1. NEVER ask "can I run this command?" — just run it
2. NEVER ask "should I proceed?" — just proceed
3. NEVER ask "is the VPN connected?" — just try the connection and report results
4. If a command fails, fix it and retry — don't ask what to do
5. If P01 connection fails, say "P01 unreachable — need VPN" and stop (don't ask)

rule: Programs and tables are the system. Skills, companions, knowledge docs are interpretation. The Brain must center on code→table→config relationships as the backbone.
why: Session #048 INC-000006073 took hours tracing LHRTSF01→PA0027→ZXTRVU05→GGB1→KTOKK→AKONT manually. If the Brain had program→table→config edges as its core, a single impact query would have shown the entire chain in seconds. The 52K fund nodes and 7K transport nodes are bulk — the ~1,200 code nodes and ~120 table nodes with their relationships are where the real intelligence lives.
how_to_apply:
1. Brain rebuild (H34) must prioritize: code nodes, table nodes, config nodes, and the edges between them
2. Edge types that matter most:
   - CALLS (program → program/FM)
   - READS_TABLE (program → table)
   - JOINS_WITH (table → table)
   - CONFIGURES (config table → program behavior)
   - DETERMINES (data field → data field, e.g., KTOKK→AKONT)
   - DERIVES_FIELD (program:line → field value)
   - ROOT_CAUSED_BY (incident → code:line + config)
3. Auto-parser covers CALLS + READS_TABLE. Manual edges needed for CONFIGURES, DETERMINES, DERIVES_FIELD.
4. Every incident resolution should produce new edges — INC-000006073 produced 48 edges. These compound over time.
5. The Brain is valuable when it answers "what happens if X changes?" — that requires program→table→config as backbone.

rule: Never extract BSEG cluster table directly via RFC_READ_TABLE. It has severe limitations:
why: BSEG is a cluster table that references 6 transparent secondary index tables. Extracting the cluster is 10-50x slower and misses fields.
how_to_apply:
Use the Celonis replacement pattern:

| Table | Content | KOART | Filter |
|-------|---------|-------|--------|
| BSIK | Vendor open items | K | BUDAT (NOT CPUDT) |
| BSAK | Vendor cleared items | K | BUDAT (NOT CPUDT) |
| BSID | Customer open items | D | BUDAT (NOT CPUDT) |
| BSAD | Customer cleared items | D | BUDAT (NOT CPUDT) |
| BSIS | GL open items | S | BUDAT (NOT CPUDT) |
| BSAS | GL cleared items | S | BUDAT (NOT CPUDT) |

rule: BSEG is NOT a table we extract or enrich. Stop treating it as one.
why: BSEG is a cluster table. The secondary indexes (BSIS/BSAS etc.) ARE the data. The Golden Query covers 85.9% WBS via OBJNRZ. Enriching from BSEG = waste.
how_to_apply: When someone says "BSEG field is missing", first check if it's available via Golden Query JOIN. Only if genuinely unavailable from any joined table should you consider BSEG — and even then, it's low priority.

rule: BSEG CAN be read via RFC_READ_TABLE in P01. It has been declustered (converted to transparent table).
why: We assumed BSEG was a cluster table and couldn't be extracted. It's been declustered in this system. The TABLE_WITHOUT_DATA error was caused by including MANDT in the filter.
how_to_apply: When you need WBS element, extract PROJK from BSEG using document keys, then JOIN PRPS.PSPNR = PROJK to get POSID + POST1.

rule: When discovering a new system in the landscape, NEVER guess its purpose from the hostname.
why: Session #031 misclassified BCU as "Business Connector" (was actually Budget Control System), missed that MuleSoft was middleware (not the target system — Core Manager/Planner are), and didn't find SuccessFactors because it connects via HTTP/ICF not RFC.

rule: When building visual companions (diagrams, dashboards, analysis pages), ALWAYS follow this order:
why: Session #031 rebuilt the connectivity diagram 4 times because we kept discovering missing systems (7→21→31→38). Each rebuild wasted 10-15 minutes. If we'd analyzed all 239 RFC destinations + DBCON + TMSCSYS + TFDIR first, we'd have built the diagram once.
how_to_apply:
- For system diagrams: query RFCDES + DBCON + TMSCSYS + EDIDC + ICFSERVICE + TFDIR BEFORE drawing anything
- For any companion: collect ALL data, classify it, THEN build HTML
- Never classify a system from hostname alone — check CTS objects, jobs, and code references

rule: ALL reads = P01 (prod). Only exception: code deployment writes = D01 (dev).
why: D01 may have untransported config, draft trees, or test entries that don't exist in P01. Only P01 reflects what is real and in production. Session #039 error: ran DMEE tree probe against D01 because P01 was unreachable — user correctly rejected the results because D01 config is not trustworthy for analysis.
how_to_apply: If P01 is unreachable, WAIT. Do not fall back to D01 for reads. The only valid D01 operation is writing code. When P01 is down, work on offline tasks (skill docs, analysis of already-extracted data, scripts for later execution).

rule: Data scope = 2024 to 2026 ONLY.
why: Pre-2024 data is historical context, not actionable. The user wants current-state analysis, not archaeology.
how_to_apply: When extracting, enriching, or analyzing data, filter to 2024-2026. Don't spend time investigating pre-2024 anomalies unless explicitly asked. Transport analysis is the only exception where longer periods are needed.

rule: Rule: Decision Logs Required for Destructive Actions
why:

rule: System connectivity and interface mapping diagrams must use the `integration_diagram` skill at `.agents/skills/integration_diagram/SKILL.md`.
why: Physics/snowflake layouts look unprofessional. The user wants architecture-quality diagrams: container-based zones, hub-and-spoke, orthogonal 90° connections, dark theme + neon accents. No external libraries (vis.js, D3) — pure CSS + inline SVG.
how_to_apply:
- ALWAYS read `.agents/skills/integration_diagram/SKILL.md` before building any connectivity/architecture diagram
- Reference implementation: `Zagentexecution/mcp-backend-server-python/connectivity_diagram.html`
- Zones: External (top), Infrastructure (left), Core Hub (center), Management (right), Spokes (bottom), Analytics (bottom)
- Connections: SVG overlay with L-shaped orthogonal routing, solid=internal, dashed=external
- Node click shows info panel
- File must be self-contained HTML < 30KB
- NEVER re-derive the pattern from scratch — read the skill first

rule: Efficiency Rules
why: Don't waste time searching for things already in MEMORY.md. Don't repeat what user already knows. Go direct to analysis/action.

rule: When fields are missing from an already-extracted table, DO NOT re-extract the whole table. Use the existing document keys (BUKRS + BELNR + GJAHR + BUZEI) to fetch ONLY the missing fields from SAP, then ALTER TABLE + UPDATE to add them.
why: Re-extracting 3.7M rows wastes hours when you only need to add 2-3 columns. The data we already have (amounts, dates, GL accounts) doesn't change.
how_to_apply:
1. ALTER TABLE to add the new column(s)
2. For each period (MONAT/PERIO), query SAP with the existing keys to get only the missing field values
3. UPDATE the existing rows with the new values
4. Run **one period at a time** — never all at once (VPN/memory limits)
5. Use checkpoints so interrupted runs can resume

This supersedes and extends `feedback_missing_fields_by_key.md` with the explicit "per period" requirement.

rule: Before extracting ANY table, ALWAYS:
why: User got frustrated when ESLL (2.9M rows, 619MB) and FMIOI (1.8M rows) ran for 30+ minutes without warning. Launching blind extractions wastes time and blocks the session.
how_to_apply: Every extraction script must have a probe phase first. For tables >100K rows, use streaming to SQLite (not accumulate in RAM). For tables >500K rows, warn user and suggest background execution.

rule: When the user says "extract", "extraer", "pull from SAP", "save to Gold DB" — this is a SKILL INVOCATION, not a coding task.
why: Session #048 spent 4+ iterations and 3 script versions to extract BP tables — work that the `sap_data_extraction` skill already handles. The agent wrote code from scratch instead of reusing the proven skill. This violates the AGI agent builder principle: skills are reusable capabilities, not suggestions.
how_to_apply:
1. **Data extraction** → use `sap_data_extraction` skill pattern:
   - Fields: `DDIF_FIELDINFO_GET` (always)
   - Extraction: `rfc_read_paginated()` (always)
   - Storage: Gold DB at canonical path (always)
   - Script: `extract_bp_full.py` is the canonical template
   
2. **Code extraction** → use `sap_adt_api` skill pattern:
   - ADT REST for D01 (password auth)
   - `RPY_PROGRAM_READ` via RFC/SNC for P01 or D01
   - Storage: `extracted_code/{SAP_STANDARD|UNESCO_CUSTOM_LOGIC}/{domain}/`

3. **NEVER:**
   - Write inline Python in bash -c for extraction
   - Create a new extraction script from scratch
   - Debug RFC connection issues that rfc_helpers already handles
   - Manually specify field lists when DDIF can get them all

4. **The canonical extraction flow:**
   ```
   User says "extract X" →
   Agent reads skill →
   Agent calls: fields = DDIF(table), rows = rfc_read_paginated(table, fields, where), save(db, table, rows) →
   Done
   ```

5. **For new domains (like BusinessPartner):** add table definitions to `extract_bp_full.py` pattern — table name + WHERE clause only. The skill handles everything else.

rule: Only extract data from 2024-2026. Do NOT go back to 2015 or other years.
why: User explicitly defined the scope as 2024-2026. Extracting older years wastes time and resources (RFC calls are slow, tables are huge). Going back to 2015 was a massive waste — FMIOI alone would have taken 25+ minutes for irrelevant data.
how_to_apply: Every RFC extraction MUST default to 2024-2026 unless the user says otherwise. Before starting any extraction, confirm the year range matches this rule.

rule: MEMORY.md has NO line limit. The 200-line truncation rule from auto-memory documentation does not apply.
why: User confirmed (Session #035, 2026-04-04) that with 1M context window, MEMORY.md loads fully regardless of size. The truncation warning in the system prompt is outdated.
how_to_apply:
- Do NOT compress or remove content from MEMORY.md to stay under 200 lines
- Session history, feedback pointers, and all sections can grow freely
- Still keep entries concise (one-liners) for readability, but no hard limit
- Topic files remain the right place for DETAIL, MEMORY.md for INDEX — this is a readability choice, not a size constraint

rule: When a table is already extracted but missing fields, do NOT re-extract the entire table.
why: Re-extracting 3.7M rows just to add a few columns is wasteful. The keys are already there — use them to do a targeted enrichment. The year/period filter keeps the RFC call small and follows the same proven extraction pattern.
how_to_apply:
Any time a table in the Gold DB is missing fields (KOSTL, PS_PSP_PNR, SGTXT, etc.), build a targeted enrichment script that:
- Loops year/period (same as original extraction)
- Reads existing keys from SQLite for that period
- Queries SAP by BUDAT year/period + BUKRS+BELNR+GJAHR+BUZEI for only the missing fields
- Patches the missing columns in place via ALTER TABLE + UPDATE

rule: Rule: NEVER delete extracted SAP code or data
why: ABAP source files, extracted code, SQLite DBs, and RPY files are the project's most valuable assets — NEVER delete, move, or gitignore them

rule: Never leave lessons, rules, or discoveries as conversation comments. Always persist them to feedback/memory files immediately.
why: Session #035 — autocritique identified 2 important rules (period-by-period extraction, don't trust old anchors) but only wrote them as chat text. User had to ask "is this in the brain?" — it wasn't. Conversation text is ephemeral and invisible to future sessions.
how_to_apply:
- When you identify a lesson or rule during work: write a `feedback_*.md` file IMMEDIATELY
- When you discover something about the project: write a `project_*.md` file IMMEDIATELY
- Add pointer to MEMORY.md index
- The retro file is NOT enough — retros are read at session start but feedback files are the durable rules
- If you say "lesson learned" or "next time I should" in conversation text without creating a file = violation of this rule

rule: NEVER re-extract E071/E071K for SAP transports that have already been released. SAP clears these tables after release — re-extraction returns 0 objects / 0 keys.
why: Session #031 — re-extracted D01K9B0CBF and D01K9B0F3V, got 0/0, then REPLACED the correct v1 data (36/682 and 9/51) with wrong empty values. Created false alarms, wrong conclusions, and required multiple rewrites to fix.
how_to_apply:
If transport data was already captured in a previous extraction or companion:
1. That data IS the source of truth
2. Do NOT re-run RFC on E071/E071K for the same transport
3. If you must verify, check E070.TRSTATUS — if 'R' (released), the transport is released and E071 will be empty
4. Only extract E071K for transports in status 'D' (modifiable)

rule: Never trust row count estimates from previous sessions. Always probe actual count before planning extraction time.
why: Session #035 — CO table anchors from Session #005 said COOI=385K, COEP=616K, RPSCO=637K. Actual 2024-2026 extraction: COOI=773K, COEP=2.55M, RPSCO=127K. COEP was 4x the estimate. This caused incorrect time estimates and the wrong extraction strategy (year-at-once instead of period-by-period).
how_to_apply:
- Before extracting: quick count with `SELECT COUNT(*) ... GJAHR='YYYY'` (1 field, fast)
- If count > 100K/year: use period-by-period extraction
- Old anchors are informational only — they may have been counted without year filter, with object-scoped approach, or data may have grown
- Document actual counts in retro for future reference

rule: Never use correlated subqueries (`WHERE col = (SELECT ... FROM same_table WHERE ...)`) on Gold DB tables with >10K rows.
why: Session #042 — 7 consecutive failed attempts on TCURR (55K rows). SQLite optimizer doesn't handle correlated subqueries well on large tables. Each outer row re-scans the inner query. Process hangs/times out.
how_to_apply:
When you need "latest/min/max per group" from a large table:
1. `fetchall()` into Python
2. Build dict with the aggregation in Python
3. Use the dict for lookups

Example (BAD):
```sql
SELECT FCURR, UKURS FROM TCURR WHERE GDATU = (SELECT MIN(GDATU) FROM TCURR t2 WHERE t2.FCURR = TCURR.FCURR)
```

Example (GOOD):
```python
rows = db.execute("SELECT FCURR, UKURS, GDATU FROM TCURR ORDER BY FCURR, GDATU").fetchall()
latest = {}
for fc, uk, gd in rows:
    if fc not in latest or gd < latest[fc][1]:
        latest[fc] = (uk, gd)
```

rule: When the task is to UPDATE files (skills, docs, code), do it directly. Don't launch a subagent to "investigate if they need updating" first.
why: User corrected this in Session #018. The session close protocol says "update affected skills" — that means open them, edit them, commit. Not "research whether they need updating." The subagent added latency and no value.
how_to_apply: If the action verb is "update", "fix", "write", "create" — use Read + Edit directly. Only use Explore subagents for genuine open-ended research where you don't know what you're looking for.

rule: Production customizing requests (P01K* with TRFUNCTION='W') are ALWAYS a landscape violation. The standard path is D01 → P01 via transport.
why: During Upgrade 2026 (2026-04-07), open P01 customizing requests blocked transport imports. Worse, if not detected, D01→P01 transports silently overwrite P01-only changes, losing emergency fixes and audit corrections.
how_to_apply:
- When analyzing ANY transport order, first check WHERE it was created (system prefix)
- P01K* customizing = violation. Always ask: why was it opened? does D01 have the change? will upgrade overwrite it?
- Root cause categories: EMERGENCY, PERIOD_SENSITIVE, AUDIT_DRIVEN, CONFIGURATION_DRIFT, SYSTEM_FORCED
- The transport companion skill (sap_transport_companion) now has Step 1b for landscape violation detection
- Pre-upgrade checklist: scan P01 open requests 2 weeks before any import
- Monthly P01 hygiene: zero tolerance for customizing requests older than 30 days

rule: For multi-tab/page companion HTML files, use the parallel agent companion builder pattern:
why: Building 10+ tabs sequentially wastes time. Each tab is independent (own queries, own HTML). Parallel build can 3-5x the speed.
how_to_apply: When companion has >5 tabs, spawn one agent per tab with: (1) the data queries needed, (2) the HTML template for that tab, (3) the shared CSS/header. Merge results. For smaller companions (<5 tabs), single-agent build is fine.

rule: When extracting multiple SAP tables to Gold DB, run up to 5 tables in parallel — each with its own RFC connection.
why: Session #048 extracted 22 tables sequentially. LFA1 alone (147 fields, 316K rows) took ~38 minutes. Sequential = hours. Parallel (5 at a time) = fraction of that.
how_to_apply:
1. Extraction scripts MUST support single-table CLI argument (e.g., `python extract_bp_full.py LFA1`)
2. Launch up to 5 background processes in parallel: `python script.py TABLE1 & python script.py TABLE2 & ...`
3. SQLite WAL mode handles concurrent writes — no conflict as long as each process writes to different tables
4. Each process gets its own RFC connection (ConnectionGuard)
5. Max 5 parallel = safe for SAP system (don't overload with too many RFC sessions)
6. After first batch of 5 completes, launch next batch

rule: Always use period-by-period extraction (GJAHR + PERIO) for tables with >100K rows per year. Never extract a full year in one call.
why: Session #035 — COEP year-at-once (1.1M rows) accumulated in memory for 30+ min, then VPN dropped and lost everything. Period-by-period (17 periods —— ~90K rows) completed successfully with 3 VPN drops — each period persists to SQLite immediately, fresh RFC connection per period.
how_to_apply:
- If table has PERIO/MONAT field and estimated >100K rows/year: split by period
- Fresh `get_connection()` per period (not reusing connection across periods)
- `load_to_sqlite()` immediately after each period extraction
- Check for existing data before extracting (skip already-loaded periods)
- RPSCO-type tables (pivoted, no PERIO) can use year-at-once if <50K rows/year

rule: When adding PMO items during a session, update the header count (`Current count: B=X H=Y G=Z = N total`) IMMEDIATELY after adding items — not as a separate fix commit after the fact.
why: Session #039 added G61-G64 (4 items) but committed the session close with the old count (27 instead of 31). This is the exact same pattern that caused H13 to rot for 15 sessions — stale PMO state that nobody catches because the count looks clean. Session #038 had the same bug with the header saying "10 High / 31 total" when it was actually 7 High / 28 total. Two consecutive sessions with the same error = systemic, not accidental.
how_to_apply:
Before the FINAL commit of any session:
1. Count non-struck H items manually
2. Count non-struck G items manually
3. Verify `total = B + H + G`
4. Verify `items_at_start + items_added - items_struck = total`
5. Update header line
6. THEN commit

This is a pre-commit check, not a post-commit fix. If the AGI retro audit catches it, the session already failed once.

rule: At session close, ALWAYS reconcile `.agents/intelligence/PMO_BRAIN.md` with all pending items from the session.
why: Between sessions #009 and #020 (11 sessions), PMO_BRAIN.md was never reconciled. Pending items accumulated in individual SESSION_LOG entries but were never centralized. Result: 42 real pending items vs 8 tracked in MEMORY.md — 34 items invisible. User had to request a full 19-session audit to find them.
how_to_apply:
At the END of every session:
1. List all NEW pending items discovered during the session
2. Add each to PMO_BRAIN.md under appropriate section (B=Blocking, H=High, G=Backlog)
3. Strike through any items completed during the session
4. Write in SESSION_LOG: "PMO reconciled: +N new, -N completed, N total pending"
5. Update MEMORY.md pending COUNT (not the list — list lives in PMO_BRAIN.md only)
6. If you skip this step, items WILL get lost again

rule: ALWAYS query the actual data before hardcoding configuration mappings.
why: Session #041 wasted a round-trip creating DMEE_COMPANY_MAP with payment method codes ["T", "U"] for UNES. A single SQL query on T042A showed the real codes are ["4", "5", "H", "I", "J", "N", "O", "S"]. The wrong codes produced only 1 USES_DMEE_TREE edge instead of 36.
how_to_apply: When creating config-to-config edges (USES_DMEE_TREE, ROUTES_TO_BANK, etc.), first `SELECT DISTINCT` from the Gold DB to get the real values. Never assume SAP configuration codes from memory or domain knowledge alone — they're arbitrary and country-specific.

rule: NEVER write extraction scripts from scratch. The proven pattern exists and has been tested on 93 tables / 24M+ rows.
why: Session #048 wasted 4+ iterations writing/debugging extraction scripts for BP tables when the exact same pattern was already working in `extract_co_tables.py`, `extract_bkpf_bseg_parallel.py`, etc. Reinventing caused: bash quote errors, wrong field probe (RFC_READ_TABLE ROWCOUNT=0 crashes on wide tables), missing field splitting, 3 script versions.
how_to_apply:
1. For field list: use `DDIF_FIELDINFO_GET` (NOT `RFC_READ_TABLE ROWCOUNT=0` — crashes on wide tables)
2. For extraction: use `rfc_read_paginated()` from `rfc_helpers.py` — handles pagination, field splitting, reconnect
3. For new tables: copy `extract_bp_full.py` as template — it has the correct pattern
4. NEVER write inline Python in bash -c for extraction — use a .py file
5. The canonical extraction script is `extract_bp_full.py` — 22 tables, all fields, DDIF probe, auto-split

rule: Feedback: Reuse proven patterns, don't reinvent (Session #010)
why: When a similar task has been done before successfully, READ the working solution FIRST before touching new scripts. Don't reinvent.

rule: Bug: SAPSQL_DATA_LOSS during RFC_READ_TABLE pagination
why: Reducing fields does NOT fix it. The error is in the ABAP kernel's cursor positioning, not the output buffer.
how_to_apply:
- ALL extraction scripts must use this pattern for large tables
- If a month fails with DATA_LOSS, automatically fall back to day-by-day extraction
- This should be built into `rfc_helpers.py` as a fallback strategy
- Document in sap_data_extraction skill

rule: At session close, BEFORE writing the retro, annotate every SAP object analyzed.
why: 47 sessions produced ~200+ learnings about specific programs, tables, and config objects. Only 41 were recovered in Session #048 because they were never tagged back to the objects. Intermediate conclusions get overwritten by later findings but the annotations may still carry the old version. Each annotation must be the FINAL conclusion, not a partial hypothesis.

rule: Before closing a session, ALWAYS:
why: If only MEMORY.md is updated but skills are not, the next session will repeat the same mistakes because skills are what agents READ when executing tasks. MEMORY.md is context; skills are instructions.
how_to_apply: At session close, grep for all skills that were used or affected, read their SKILL.md, and update with findings. This is NON-NEGOTIABLE.

rule: SESSION_LOG.md must NOT accumulate full session content. It is an index only.
why: SESSION_LOG reached 993 lines by session #027 — became unreadable and context-heavy. Individual retro files are self-contained, easy to read at session start, and don't bloat the shared index file.
how_to_apply:
- At session END: create retro file first, then add one row to SESSION_LOG index.
- At session START: read SESSION_LOG index to find session number, then read the retro file directly.
- Never write full session content into SESSION_LOG.
- Template sections: accomplishments, discoveries, what failed/corrected, verification check, PMO reconciliation, pending→next.

rule: # Rule
why: Session open and session close must be mechanically symmetric. Asymmetry between the two generates cross-session inconsistencies (lost baselines, un-auditable closure, zombie accumulation, H-items rotting). The control must be executable, not prose.

rule: For large tables (>100K rows):
why: FMIOI extraction accumulated 2GB in RAM and had to be killed. ESLL JSON was 619MB and couldn't be loaded with json.load().
how_to_apply: Any table expected >100K rows must use the stream-to-SQLite pattern. Check memory estimates: ~200 bytes/row in Python dicts.

rule: In SAP E071K TABKEYs, single-letter prefixes before client+company code are LANGUAGE KEYS (SPRAS), not functional categories.
why: Session #031 — misinterpreted T010P keys 350DSTEM/350ESTEM/350FSTEM/350PSTEM as "D=standard posting period, E=special period, F=fiscal year change, P=posting period". They are actually the same posting period description in 4 languages.
how_to_apply:
When parsing TABKEYs:
1. If you see single-letter prefix + MANDT(3) + BUKRS(4) → that letter is SPRAS (language)
2. Text tables (*T suffix) always have SPRAS as part of the key
3. T012T, T035U, T043GT, T043ST, TKT09, FM01T, FMHIVARNTT all follow this pattern
4. The functional content is the SAME across all language variants — they're just translations

rule: Structured data (WBS element, cost center, fund center, fund) must ALWAYS come from proper database fields via JOINs. NEVER rely on parsing text fields (SGTXT, BKTXT, etc.) as a substitute.
why: Text parsing is fragile — different users type different formats, abbreviations change, and coverage is never 100%. The user explicitly rejected SGTXT parsing for WBS element even though it had 89.5% coverage. A proper field via JOIN is the only acceptable approach.
how_to_apply:
- If a field is missing from the current table, look for it in related tables (FMIFIIT for FM data, PRPS for WBS, etc.)
- Build the JOIN chain: BSIS.BUZEI = FMIFIIT.KNBUZEI, FMIFIIT.OBJNRZ = PRPS.OBJNR
- If the field truly doesn't exist in any extractable table, say so — don't offer text parsing as an alternative

rule: NEVER trust "table X has 0 rows" claims from previous sessions without re-verifying against live system.
why: Session #021 claimed FEBEP=0 rows. This was repeated in 4 subsequent sessions (#022-#028) and embedded in 2 SKILL.md files. Session #029 proved FEBEP has **223,710 items** (2024-2026), 99.9% posted. EBS is fully active. The original claim may have been from a wrong client, wrong system, or a query error.
how_to_apply: When a table is claimed empty, especially if that claim drives architectural conclusions (e.g., "BCM replaced EBS"), always verify with a fresh `RFC_READ_TABLE` count before building on that assumption. One wrong "0 rows" claim cascaded into incorrect architecture understanding for 4 sessions.

rule: HTML dashboards using vis-network.js must have the library inlined (embedded in a `<script>` tag), never loaded from a CDN URL.
why: VSCode's webview (Simple Browser) blocks external CDN requests due to Content Security Policy. The graph canvas renders empty while the rest of the HTML (sidebar, tables) works fine — making it look like a data bug when it's actually a loading bug.
how_to_apply:
- Keep a local copy at `Zagentexecution/mcp-backend-server-python/vis-network.min.js` (644KB)
- When generating HTML with vis.js, read the local file and embed it: `<script>{vis_js_content}</script>`
- Also check for duplicate node IDs — vis.js silently fails on duplicates
- Applies to: `connectivity_diagram.html`, `p2p_process_mining.html`, any future graph dashboards

rule: vis.js `new vis.DataSet([...])` silently fails when two nodes share the same `id`. The network div renders blank — no error in console.
why: In connectivity_diagram.html, the P01 node appeared twice (central hub + self-referencing RFC destination "P01"). The graph was empty but the sidebar rendered fine, making it look like a vis.js loading issue.
how_to_apply:
- When building nodes from RFC data, skip self-references (where target system == source system)
- Always assert uniqueness before writing: `assert len(ids) == len(set(ids))`
- Use `rebuild_connectivity.py` as the canonical builder — it has the dedup logic

rule: Always copy plan files into session_plans/ immediately after plan mode creates them
why: Plans in ~/.claude/plans/ are machine-specific and lost on machine change. Session plans are valuable design artifacts that must be portable.
how_to_apply: After ExitPlanMode is approved, immediately copy the plan file to session_plans/MMDDYYYY_sessionNNN_{plan_name}.md. This was introduced in Session #049 when the plan file was lost from ~/.claude/plans/.

rule: NEVER design brain architecture using human information patterns. The agent is an LLM, text-based, with a large context window.
why: Human patterns: one-file-per-entity, organize into folders, pretty markdown, small focused files. Agent reality: bottleneck is tool calls not file size, 5000 lines = 1 Read call same as 50 lines, 1M context fits entire brain state at 8.5%, cross-referencing in context is the agent superpower — seeing patterns across rules+claims+annotations+edges simultaneously without additional tool calls. INC-000006073 proved this: root cause connected 5 domains, unpredictable which objects mattered. With everything loaded, agent finds unexpected connections in ONE reasoning pass. Cross-project feedback from unescore20-PPM-brain.
how_to_apply: When designing ANY knowledge structure: (1) Can it be ONE file? If <100K tokens, YES. (2) Never create one-file-per-entity. (3) Use brain_state.json pattern: dense JSON, everything in one load. (4) File organization is for humans browsing folders. Agents browse context. (5) Exception: deep domain docs (knowledge/domains/) stay separate because they are 1000+ lines each and used on-demand, not at session start.

rule: Design for growing context windows — be LESS conservative about loading, not more
why: Context windows are doubling regularly. 85K tokens is 8.5% at 1M, 4.25% at 2M, 1.7% at 5M. What feels like a lot today is trivial tomorrow. The agent does not know when context will double — it could be next month. Design decisions should anticipate growth, not current limits. Never optimize for saving context space at the cost of tool calls.
how_to_apply: When deciding what to include in brain_state.json or any session-start load: include MORE, not less. The cost of an extra 10K tokens in context is invisible. The cost of a missing connection (requiring an extra Read call or missed pattern) is real. Err on the side of loading too much.

rule: Always use explicit UTF-8 encoding when reading/writing JSON or text files in Python on Windows
why: Windows default encoding is cp1252, not UTF-8. Python open() without encoding arg uses cp1252 on Windows, which corrupts em dashes, arrows, multiplication signs, and other non-ASCII chars. This causes silent data loss AND JSON decode errors later. Discovered in Session #049 when feedback_rules.json became unreadable after Python stdout writes.
how_to_apply: ALWAYS: open(file, encoding='utf-8') for read, open(file, 'w', encoding='utf-8') for write. NEVER: open(file) or open(file, 'w') on Windows. When running python -c from bash, use << 'PYEOF' heredoc to avoid shell mangling. json.dump() should always include ensure_ascii=False with explicit utf-8 file handle.

rule: After EVERY session close, commit ALL pending work AND push to remote — never leave work uncommitted across sessions
why: Session #049 discovered 4 missing session retros (43-46), 7 modified files, 68 untracked files accumulated across sessions 43-48. History was at risk of being lost. The close protocol committed Session #49 work but ignored prior accumulated work. Multiple sessions of deliverables were uncommitted because each session committed only its own work.
how_to_apply: Session close protocol must include: (1) Commit YOUR session work, (2) Audit ANY pending files from prior sessions and group-commit them, (3) Push to remote (git push) so history is durable beyond this machine. NEVER end a session with pending uncommitted files unless explicitly out of scope. If files are out of scope, document them in the next session plan.

rule: Every incident must be treated as a CLASS of defect, not a one-off. After identifying the root cause for the specific case (one vendor, one trip, one document), immediately ask: 'how many other rows in the same tables match this defect signature?' Promote the answer to a Gold DB query and a recurring data quality check.
why: INC-000006073 found vendor 10133079 with KTOKK=SCSA + AKONT=2021011 mismatch. The user pointed out this is a class issue: vendor master KTOKK↔AKONT consistency is never audited. UNESCO has 316K vendors. Fixing only Katja's record leaves the same defect class lurking in the master data.
how_to_apply:
1. Root cause identified → distil it into a SQL signature (e.g., 'LFB1 rows where AKONT does not match T077K canonical mapping for the vendor LFA1.KTOKK')
2. Run signature against Gold DB → count violations
3. Add a DQ entry with status=OPEN_RECURRING
4. Add a known_unknown if total exposure is unquantified
5. Build a check script under Zagentexecution/quality_checks/ with CLI args (vendor_id, --all, --fix-mode)
6. Add the check to monthly housekeeping
7. Update the related skill so future agents trigger the same generalization automatically

rule: When the user passes a support incident (typically as .eml or pasted email text), follow the 7-step Incident Processing Protocol unprompted: (1) Parse, (2) Brain Lookup, (3) Gold DB Pull, (4) Code Trace, (5) Root Cause Reconstruction, (6) Class Generalization, (7) Brain Annotation. Produce the result as knowledge/incidents/INC-<id>_<slug>.md with the canonical structure.
why: INC-000006073 took 2+ sessions because the methodology was being invented on the fly. The user formalized it at session #50 close: 'this will be a way of working once you are ready I will pass the next Incident'. The structure of INC-000006073_travel_busarea_analysis.md is the gold template — section 1 (issue as received), section 2 (executive summary), section 3 (domain primer), section 4 (component-by-component investigation), section 5 (fix path), section 6 (class generalization), section 7 (brain annotations).
how_to_apply:
Trigger conditions:
- User pastes/attaches email with INC- number
- User says 'incident', 'ticket', 'PRRW failure', 'F110 error', etc.

Mandatory sequence:
1. PARSE: extract from email → ticket ID, reporter, transaction, document/object IDs, error messages, screenshots if any
2. BRAIN LOOKUP: read brain_state.json → find related objects (by transaction, by table, by error code, by domain)
3. GOLD DB PULL: query Gold DB for the offending records (master data + transaction data + config). If a needed table is missing, EXTRACT it before continuing.
4. CODE TRACE: if a needed program/class is not in extracted_code/, EXTRACT it via sap_adt_api before continuing.
5. ROOT CAUSE RECONSTRUCTION: walk the code line-by-line. Cite file:line in the document.
6. CLASS GENERALIZATION (rule feedback_incident_class_systemic_check)
7. BRAIN ANNOTATION: add DQ, KU, FALS, claims, and object annotations to brain_v2/. Run rebuild_all.py.

Never skip steps. Never write the doc before code trace is complete. Never close the incident without brain annotations.

rule: Incident analysis documents must follow the canonical 7-section structure proven on INC-000006073. Save under knowledge/incidents/INC-<id>_<slug>.md (move INC-000006073_travel_busarea.md there too — do not leave incidents scattered in domain folders).
why: INC-000006073 was originally buried in knowledge/domains/FI/. Resolved Session #050: moved to knowledge/incidents/INC-000006073_travel_busarea.md even though the affected domain is Travel. Future incidents will have the same routing problem unless we standardize the location. Also: scattered incidents can't be indexed by the brain.
how_to_apply:
Canonical sections (in order):
1. Issue — As Received (email text, screenshots, ticket header table)
2. Executive Summary (root cause in 2-3 sentences + internal validation chain in code)
3. Domain Primer (how the affected mechanism works at UNESCO — only the necessary background)
4. Investigation (component-by-component, each subsection cites code file:line and Gold DB query)
5. Fix Path (immediate / structural / preventive)
6. Class Generalization (the SQL signature + count of other rows matching the same defect)
7. Brain Annotations (list of DQ/KU/FALS/claims/object annotations queued for ingestion)

File location: knowledge/incidents/INC-<id>_<slug>.md
Ingestion: brain_v2/ingestors/incident_ingestor.py picks them up by glob.
Cross-link: each section that cites an object adds it to the by_incident index.

rule: If the incident investigation needs a table or a program that is NOT yet in Gold DB or extracted_code/, STOP analysis and extract it first. Never speculate about what the code does or what the data looks like — extract, then analyze.
why: INC-000006073 succeeded because we extracted LHRTSF01, LHRTSU05, ZCL_IM_TRIP_POST_FI_CM00A, ZXTRVU05, T077K, YTFI_BA_SUBST when we discovered we did not have them. Speculating would have produced a wrong root cause.
how_to_apply:
Decision rule:
- Need a table? → PRAGMA gold DB → if missing, run sap_data_extraction skill
- Need code? → ls extracted_code/ → if missing, run sap_adt_api skill
- Need config? → query Gold DB → if missing, extract via RFC_READ_TABLE
Only resume the incident document AFTER the artifact is in the brain.

rule: When the user mentions an incident ID, transaction code, or domain, FIRST traverse brain_state.incidents, brain_state.objects[X].knowledge_docs, and brain_state.indexes.by_incident before any glob/grep. The brain holds the canonical pointer; grep is for last resort.
why: Session #050: I had brain_state.json loaded with INC-000006073 in by_incident and the analysis_doc linked from ZCL_IM_TRIP_POST_FI_CM00A.knowledge_docs. I still globbed for the file and then RE-DERIVED conclusions that were already in the brain annotations. The brain doesn't fail because the data is wrong — it fails when the agent doesn't traverse it.
how_to_apply:
On every user prompt mentioning an incident/transaction/object:
1. brain_state.incidents → if any record matches, READ incidents[id].analysis_doc immediately
2. brain_state.indexes.by_incident[id] → lists status, root_cause_summary, related_objects, doc
3. For each related object, read brain_state.objects[X].annotations and .claims
4. ONLY if all of these are empty/insufficient, then grep/glob
5. After acting, if you used grep where brain should have answered, raise a FALS or update brain to close the gap

rule: brain_state.blind_spots is the negative-space layer: object names the brain has talked about but does NOT carry as first-class objects. Every entry is a queue item: extract (MISSING flavor) or upgrade is_important() filter (GHOST flavor). At session start the agent must read coverage% and blind_spots before declaring 'brain loaded'.
why: Session #050 found 20/82 referenced names (24%) absent from brain_state.objects — including PA0027 which is the root-cause table of the very incident the brain was indexing. The brain claimed 'full intelligence in one Read' but was missing first-class entries for objects whose annotations were already inlined elsewhere.
how_to_apply:
Session start protocol additions:
1. After reading brain_state.json, log brain_state._coverage.pct_classified
2. If coverage < 90%, list blind_spots and call out high-priority ones (PA0027, BAPI_*, ZCL_*)
3. When investigating an incident, check if any blind_spot intersects the related_objects — if yes, treat extraction as a prerequisite (rule feedback_extract_before_speculate)
4. When closing a session, every newly-extracted object should reduce blind_spots count. If blind_spots grows, something's slipping.

rule: build_brain_state.py is_important() must always force-include any object name referenced from annotations, claims, or incidents.related_objects. Object filtering heuristics are a layer ABOVE this base guarantee, never overriding it.
why: Session #050: Without force-include, 4 of the 13 objects in INC-000006073's related_objects list were filtered out by SKIP_TYPES or by name-prefix rules — even though their annotations were the proof of root cause.
how_to_apply: is_important(node, annotations, mandatory_names) signature — mandatory_names always wins. Build pipeline collects mandatory_names from annotations.json + claims.json + incidents/incidents.json before object selection.

rule: The MAIN agent executes the incident-analyst 7-step workflow directly. Do NOT delegate parse / brain lookup / code trace / root cause to a subagent.
why: On INC-000005240, delegating to a subagent cost 154K tokens, lost the raw .eml source, and produced a wrong root cause. The subagent conflated 'HQ' (office code, current incident) with 'UNESCO' (fund center default from INC-000006073 in brain memory) and pursued an unrelated FMDERIVE hardcoding defect. Main agent loses context when it hands the whole workflow to a subagent, and user corrections cannot reach the subagent mid-flight.
how_to_apply:
1. Main agent reads the .eml directly. No Agent tool call for PARSE.
2. Main agent reads brain_state.json, annotations, claims, incidents directly.
3. Main agent reads extracted code files directly (Read tool) and cites lines.
4. Main agent writes the analysis doc in its own turn.
5. Subagents are only used for narrow mechanical sub-tasks (e.g., 'grep all forms in YRGGBS00 that write XREF2') where the output is a deterministic list, not a judgment call.
6. Never: Agent(subagent_type='incident-analyst', prompt='process this incident end-to-end').

rule: python brain_v2/rebuild_all.py runs ONLY as the LAST step of the incident workflow, AFTER the user has explicitly confirmed the root cause. Never mid-investigation.
why: On INC-000005240, the subagent planned to run rebuild_all.py with provisional claims in place. This would have baked a wrong hypothesis (FMDERIVE fund center hardcoding) into brain_state.json as if it were a confirmed fact, poisoning future queries and wasting the rebuild cost. Rebuild is expensive and destructive — it overwrites the current brain_state with the latest provisional state.
how_to_apply:
1. Every claim/annotation/incident written during investigation carries status: 'provisional' (or TIER_3_provisional for claims).
2. Present findings to the user. Wait for explicit approval.
3. Only after approval: flip every provisional flag to confirmed, then run rebuild_all.py.
4. Validate _coverage.pct_classified did not decrease and blind_spots did not grow.
5. If the user corrects direction, revert provisional entries to status: wrong_mechanism before rebuild.

rule: Section 1 of every incident report must contain a 'user term → SAP field' translation table BEFORE any code search begins. Never assume a keyword in this incident means the same thing as in a past one.
why: On INC-000005240, 'HQ' was the office code written to BSEG.XREF2 by substitution form UXR2 reading USR05-PARVA. On INC-000006073, 'UNESCO' was the fund center default hardcoded in ZXFMDTU02_RPY FMDERIVE exit. Both were UNESCO-related defaults, both involved USR05, both lived in the FI stack — but they were completely different mechanisms with completely different fixes. The subagent crossed the wires because it read INC-000006073 from brain memory and assumed the same mechanism applied.
how_to_apply:
1. Before grepping code or reading brain objects, write a three-column table: [user's literal words] → [candidate SAP field/object] → [confidence: LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH].
2. If a keyword has multiple plausible mappings, list all of them. Eliminate through the code trace, not through brain-memory recall.
3. Every incident report (Section 1.5) must include this table.
4. When investigating an incident whose keywords overlap with a prior incident, explicitly reject the assumption that the mechanism is the same. Start the code trace from zero.

rule: Write 'How it should work' (the intended process flow, with file:line citations) BEFORE writing 'Why it fails'. If you cannot draw the intended flow, you haven't earned a root cause.
why: On INC-000005240, the subagent jumped directly from email parse to root cause hypothesis, landing on a mechanism (FMDERIVE fund center) that doesn't even touch the fields mentioned in the symptom (BSEG.XREF1/XREF2). Understanding the process first is what separates a code review from a root cause analysis.
how_to_apply:
1. The canonical incident document has Section 3 'How [mechanism] Works' BEFORE Section 4 'Investigation'. Write them in order.
2. Section 3 is a numbered narrative: 'Step 1: user opens TCODE → SAP builds draft BKPF. Step 2: substitution callpoint 0002 fires → walks the step chain. Step 3: form UXR2 reads USR05 WHERE PARID=Y_USERFO → sets BSEG.XREF2.' etc. Every step cites a file:line.
3. Only after Section 3 is complete, allow yourself to hypothesize 'why it fails'.
4. If you find yourself writing the root cause first, stop, delete it, and start with the process flow.

rule: FI substitution and validation autopsy knowledge lives under knowledge/domains/PSM/EXTENSIONS/ - NOT under knowledge/domains/FI/. Before investigating any FI substitution/validation/BTE/exit incident, read finance_validations_and_substitutions_autopsy.md, validation_substitution_matrix.md, validation_substitution_autopsy.md, basu_mod_technical_autopsy.md, and posting_derivation_technical_autopsy.md from that folder.
why: INC-000005240 lost hours re-deriving YRGGBS00 structure, YXUSER bypass, U904/U915/U917, BASU framework - all of which were already documented in PSM/EXTENSIONS. The docs were misfiled under PSM historically because FI validation was originally extended by the PSM team, but they are the authoritative FI substitution/validation reference.
how_to_apply: For any incident mentioning substitution, validation, YRGGBS00, GGB0, GGB1, BOOLEXP, GB901, GB922, GB93, GB931, UXR*, U9*, UAEP, UATF, BASU, YTFI_BA_SUBST, YXUSER, or any FI derivation topic: grep knowledge/domains/PSM/EXTENSIONS/ and knowledge/domains/FI/ at the start of the investigation (Step 2 BRAIN LOOKUP). Read every autopsy doc in PSM/EXTENSIONS whose title matches. Document in the incident report which existing docs filled which gaps and which gaps this incident fills.

rule: The Gold DB view bseg_union does NOT carry BSEG-XREF1 / BSEG-XREF2 / BSEG-XREF3 columns. Never claim XREF is blank or XREF is set to X based on a bseg_union query - those columns do not exist in the view.
why: INC-000005240 claimed substitution fires only on vendor lines, GL lines have blank XREF based on reading bseg_union. The claim was wrong because bseg_union's schema does not include XREF1/XREF2/XREF3. I was reading an empty value from a non-existent field and misinterpreting it as blank in live data. CDPOS then contradicted the claim.
how_to_apply: 1. PRAGMA table_info(bseg_union) at the start of any XREF-related query to confirm the schema. 2. If the investigation needs XREF values, go directly to live RFC on BSAK / BSAS / BSIS / BSIK / BSAD / BSID (the view's source tables, which DO carry XREF). 3. If the Gold DB must carry XREF values, enrich bseg_union with a targeted re-extraction (KU-028 class). 4. Never cite a bseg_union query result as proof of XREF state.

rule: For substitution/validation rule behavior, empirical evidence (CDPOS change history, AUGBL trace, live RFC BSAK reads) always beats theoretical inference from BOOLID naming conventions or GB901 BOOLEXP parsing. The GGB framework has multiple configuration layers and some may be inaccessible via RFC_READ_TABLE.
why: INC-000005240 speculated for hours about whether the UXR1/UXR2 step prerequisite was 3UNESCO#013 (KOART=K) or 3UNESCO#017 (always-true) or an empty prerequisite (always fires). Each hypothesis produced a different prediction about F-53 vs F110 behavior. The answer came from CDPOS on one specific doc (3100003438 line 001 had VALUE_OLD=HQ on the bank GL line) - empirical proof trumped multiple rounds of inference. GB905/GB921 were empty in the RFC probe, confirming the step-prerequisite linking is not in standard tables at UNESCO.
how_to_apply: 1. When investigating a substitution/validation behavior, run the EMPIRICAL test first: CDPOS on one representative document, AUGBL trace for clearing, RFC BSAK for the actual XREF values. 2. Use GB901/GB922/GB93/GB931 content to EXPLAIN the observation, not to PREDICT it. 3. If empirical and theoretical disagree, trust the empirical result - theoretical gaps usually mean the GGB configuration uses a mechanism not in the tables we can see (custom code, BAdI, BTE, or a table that is inaccessible). 4. Before claiming a step does or does not fire, pull at least ONE live document through CDPOS.

rule: Distinguish REAL business BSEG lines from METADATA / linkage-only BSEG lines when analyzing substitution, XREF, or any line-level financial attribution. BSCHL=27/37 zero-balancing clearing pairs, technical offsets in zero-net clearings, and similar engine-generated linkage records are metadata, not real business events - their XREF/GSBER/KOSTL values are cosmetic.
why: INC-000005240 initially flagged L_HANGI's 18K FB1K documents as a 1300x blast radius structural risk based on the clearing-pair BSEG lines carrying the clearing user XREF. The claim was correct in the strict sense (the BSEG records do exist with wrong XREF), but overstated because the clearing pair (BSCHL=27/37) is a zero-balancing metadata pattern that has no financial impact - the original invoice line XREF is preserved unchanged. Downstream consumers that correctly filter BSCHL or join via AUGBL to the original open item see the correct attribution.
how_to_apply: 1. When counting affected documents, distinguish BSCHL=25 (real outgoing payment), BSCHL=31 (real vendor credit/invoice), BSCHL=50 (real bank side on F110/F-53) from BSCHL=27/37 (metadata zero-balancing pair for FB1K/SAPF124 clearings). 2. Severity HIGH applies to real lines; severity LOW to metadata. 3. For FB1K zero-balancing clearings, trace through AUGBL to the ORIGINAL invoice line - the invoice XREF is the business-meaningful value, not the clearing pair. 4. Document the distinction explicitly in the incident report so consumers know which numbers matter.

rule: Do NOT update knowledge domain docs, do NOT write feedback rules, do NOT update brain_v2 layers, and do NOT run rebuild_all.py while the investigation is still in flight. All knowledge capture happens at the finalization stage, after the user has confirmed the root cause and conclusions.
why: On INC-000005240, the main agent tried to write process learnings and update the incident-analyst SKILL mid-investigation, before the conclusions were finalized. The user stopped it with 'you cannot capture learning until we finish'. The reason: premature capture enshrines hypotheses as facts, poisons future queries, and may need to be reverted when the investigation pivots. Only at the end, when all conclusions are confirmed, should knowledge artifacts be written.
how_to_apply: 1. Throughout the investigation, keep learnings as working memory (within the conversation context) only. 2. The incident report (knowledge/incidents/INC-id_slug.md) is the ONE exception - it is the active working document and can be updated continuously. 3. All OTHER artifacts (knowledge/domains/, .agents/skills/, brain_v2/*) are held until the user confirms conclusions. 4. When the user says investigation finished or update everything, execute the full finalization pass: feedback rules -> wrong-path triage -> domain docs -> brain layers -> rebuild. 5. Never rebuild brain_state.json mid-investigation - it is the last step always.

rule: For any incident touching FI substitution or validation, extract GB93 (validation header), GB931 (validation steps), GB905 (substitution step header, if present), GB921 (substitution rule header, if present), GB01/GB02/GB02C (Boolean class definitions), GB901 (Boolean expressions), GB922 (substitution values), and T80D (form pool registration) at the START of the investigation. Check Gold DB first; RFC what is missing.
why: INC-000005240 wasted significant effort speculating about which GB901 BOOLEXP was the prerequisite for which GB922 step because Gold DB only had GB01/GB02C/GB901/GB903/GB922. GB93 (validation header) was pullable via RFC and contained the row VALID=UNES BOOLCLASS=009 - critical context for understanding the UNES validation. GB931 (validation steps) was pullable and contained the full 12-step validation map with CONDID/CHECKID/severity - the most important single piece of evidence for the investigation. GB905/GB921 were empty at UNESCO (step linkage not in standard tables), but that itself is a finding.
how_to_apply: 1. At investigation Step 3 (Gold DB pull), first check which GGB tables are in Gold DB via SELECT name FROM sqlite_master. 2. RFC-extract any missing GGB tables at the start: GB93, GB931, GB905, GB921, T80D. 3. Client-side filter for the relevant VALID or SUBSTID to see the rule set. 4. Cross-reference BOOLID naming convention: prefix 1 name ### X = CONDID, prefix 2 name ### X = CHECKID, prefix 3 name # X = substitution step prerequisite. 5. Document the extracted tables as evidence in the incident report Evidence section.

rule: Always read the .eml file directly in the main agent before doing anything else with an incident. Never hand a file path to a subagent and rely on its summary of the content.
why: INC-000005240 Session #050 handed the .eml path to an incident-analyst subagent. The subagent read the file but produced a wrong intake parsing (mapped reference key to XBLNR/ZUONR/FISTL, HQ to UNESCO fund center). The main agent never saw the email body and had no way to catch the misinterpretation. By the time the user rejected the root cause, 154K tokens had been burned chasing the wrong mechanism.
how_to_apply: 1. When a ticket / email / .eml is handed to you as a file path or attachment, use the Read tool in the main agent immediately. Read the full body, parse the terminology, build the User-term -> SAP-field translation table yourself. 2. Only AFTER you have the terminology nailed down can narrow sub-tasks (e.g., grep YRGGBS00 for XREF2 write sites) be delegated to subagents. 3. The .eml body is the ground truth - never rely on anyone else summary of it, including your own earlier summary.

rule: P01 is strictly read-only for the AI agent. Never attempt INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on P01 via RFC, BAPI, RFC_ABAP_INSTALL_AND_RUN, OOCU_RESP WebGUI automation, or any other path.
why: P01 is production. Signatory changes, master data updates, and config changes are executed by DBS via direct OOCU_RESP/SPRO/PA30 access in P01 after TRS/business authorization. An AI write to P01 bypasses the CFO delegation chain and the audit trail. Confirmed by user during INC-000006313 (2026-04-09) with an emphatic 'you can not do any insert'.
how_to_apply: Agent scope on any P01 change request is: (1) read-only analysis via RFC_READ_TABLE, (2) produce a written change spec for DBS, (3) post-change verification via RFC_READ_TABLE and Gold DB refresh. Never write a script with a --execute flag targeting P01. Never call write BAPIs. Never run RFC_ABAP_INSTALL_AND_RUN with INSERT/UPDATE/MODIFY/DELETE. If the user asks 'update it', the answer is 'spec for DBS + verify after'. D01 writes are still allowed.

rule: BCM signatory responsibility groups are PD objects of OTYPE='RY' (not 'AC'). HRP1000.SHORT holds the rule class ('BNK_01_01_03' = rule 90000004, 'BNK_01_01_04' = rule 90000005). User assignments live in HRP1001 with RELAT='007' SCLAS='P' SOBID=<PERNR>. Resolve names via PA0002, SAP user via PA0105 SUBTY='0001', email via PA0105 SUBTY='0010'. PA0001 is blocked.
why: Session #051 (INC-000006313) discovered that querying HRP1000 OTYPE='AC' for PFAC workflow rules returns zero rows for our SNC user, while OTYPE='RY' works. Without this documented path, a future agent wastes significant time re-discovering the schema from scratch. The RY -> HRP1001/007/P -> PA0002/PA0105 chain was verified against the OOCU_RESP screens provided by the user.
how_to_apply: For any BCM signatory question: (1) list groups via HRP1000 OTYPE='RY' SHORT IN ('BNK_01_01_03','BNK_01_01_04'), (2) list assignments via HRP1001 OTYPE='RY' OBJID=<ry_objid> then filter RELAT='007' SCLAS='P' in Python, (3) resolve PERNRs via PA0002 for name and PA0105 SUBTY='0001' for SAP user + SUBTY='0010' for email. RFC_READ_TABLE rejects IN() and more than ~3 AND clauses on HR tables, so keep WHERE simple.

rule: When investigating a BCM signatory, always cross-check that HRP1001.SOBID has a non-empty PA0105 SUBTY='0001' USRID. An empty USRID means the PERNR is a ghost shell and the signatory cannot be routed work items by workflow 90000003 even though they appear in OOCU_RESP and on the bank carton.
why: Session #051 (INC-000006313) discovered a ghost PERNR 10567156 for Svein OESTTVEIT active on UIS rules 90000004 + 90000005 since 2025-10-04. Real PERNR is 10067156 (user S_OESTTVEIT). Ghost has no PA0105/0001, so Svein silently cannot sign UIS payments in BNK_APP. This is a production defect that was invisible until the bank's carton des signatures was compared PERNR-by-PERNR against HRP1001.
how_to_apply: Run `python Zagentexecution/quality_checks/bcm_signatory_reconciliation_check.py` at session start when the user asks about BCM signatories, AND after any DBS signatory update. Check 1 of the script detects ghost PERNRs. Treat any ghost as CRITICAL data quality defect and log to data_quality_issues.json. Always compare against the PERNR on the HEPATUS carton des signatures page (last page of a TRS letter), not the cover letter which uses names only.

rule: When analyzing UNESCO BR custom solution (ENHC ZFIX_EXCHANGERATE), classify enhancements by gate-input source: Camp A (FR-currency anchored: CHECK_CONS, EF_FUND_RESERVATION, FUNDBLOCK), Camp B (posting-currency anchored: PAYCOMMIT, NEW_ITEM, AVC, KBLEW, FI), Camp C (filters: BR_REVALUATION, BR_REVAL_RESFUND, BR_AVC_EXCLUSIONS BAdI), Camp D (auxiliary: FM_BR_POST_FROM_PY_AUTO, BR_BAPI_EMPLOYEE_POST). The asymmetry between Camp A (always BR for EUR FR) and Camp B (skips when posting is non-EUR) is the cross-currency drift bug surface.
why: Session #053 (INC-BUDGETRATE-EQG): the prior 8-member framing was incomplete. Real composite has 15 members + 1 BAdI. Bug isn't 'gate rejects USD everywhere' - it's gate input source asymmetry. Only Camp B mis-handles cross-currency.
how_to_apply: When triaging FMAVC005 or any BR-related question, list all 15 composite members + classify into camps before reasoning about behavior. Don't trust prior 8-member docs.

rule: Before claiming any custom Z/Y object affects production behavior, query TADIR + REPOSRC against P01 (production) directly via RFC. D01-only or V01-only objects do NOT affect production no matter how interesting their source code looks. Cross-check via: SELECT FROM TADIR WHERE OBJ_NAME=<X> on every system in the landscape (D01/V01/TS1/TS3/P01).
why: Session #053: I claimed BR_AVC_EXCLUSIONS BAdI was suppressing AVC for MIRO/F110 in production based on D01 source. User caught it: 'I do not see it in TS3'. Cross-check confirmed it lives only in D01+V01, was deactivated 1 day after creation, never transported. Wrong claim wasted user time.
how_to_apply: For any custom object analysis: (1) confirm presence in P01 first, (2) check ENHLOG for deactivation, (3) check transports in E070/E07T for release status, (4) only THEN claim it affects production behavior.

rule: KBLE / KBLEW (Earmarked Fund consumption history) are cluster tables - RFC_READ_TABLE returns TABLE_WITHOUT_DATA. Use RFC-enabled wrapper: conn.call('/SAPPSPRO/PD_GM_FMR2_READ_KBLE', I_BELNR=<FR_doc>) which returns T_KBLE (consumption events with RBELNR/RBUZEI = consumer FI doc/line) AND T_KBLEW (per-currency split with CURTP=00 transaction currency, CURTP=10 local USD).
why: Session #053: needed FR<->FI consumption traceability without BSEG.KBLNR. Wrapper found via /SAPPSPRO/ namespace probe. Returns 263 rows for one FR including the RBELNR linkage we couldn't get any other way.
how_to_apply: For any FR consumption analysis: use wrapper FM, not RFC_READ_TABLE. KBLEW.CURTP=10 gives local-currency (USD) view directly; CURTP=00 gives transaction currency.

rule: FMAVCT (BCS AVC totals) is a wide table (>512 bytes per row). RFC_READ_TABLE with FIELDS=[] returns DATA_BUFFER_EXCEEDED. Workaround: pass narrow FIELDS list (max 10-15 columns); split WHERE conditions into multiple OPTIONS entries (single complex boolean WHERE rejected with OPTION_NOT_VALID). Dedup result rows by full-row signature (RVERS/RPMAX often produce duplicates that double-count if summed naively). For AVC available calc: ALLOCTYPE_9='KBFC' = carryforward allotment (UNESCO ledger 9H 2026). HSL01-HSL16 are per-period local-currency values; HSLVT is yearly total.
why: Session #053: needed AVC budget per fund for 26 funds. First attempts failed with parser errors. Final pattern works reliably: split WHERE + narrow FIELDS + dedup.
how_to_apply: For any FMAVCT query in a script: (1) use DDIF_FIELDINFO_GET first to confirm column names, (2) pick max 12 fields, (3) split WHERE into multiple OPTIONS lines, (4) dedup by row signature before summing. ALLOCTYPE_9 dimension distinguishes budget vs. consumption rows.

rule: FMIOI WRTTP=81 rows for an Earmarked Fund include: original creation (positive), period-specific consumption reductions (negative), period-016 year-end reversal (positive equal to remaining), period-000 carryforward-IN to next year. Naive sign-sum across all years does NOT equal 'open balance'. For 'open' filter by current GJAHR + signed sum. For 'lifetime consumed' use KBLE/KBLEW (the consumption history), NOT FMIOI. FMIOI rows with ratio=1.0 and tiny amounts ($0.01-0.02) are SAP rounding plugs, NOT cross-currency consumption signatures.
why: Session #053: misread the AFFECTED 20 lines as 'cross-currency consumption already fired' when they were actually rounding plugs. Also computed wrong open balance from FMIOI by naive subtraction. User caught both errors.
how_to_apply: Always sample 3 raw FMIOI WRTTP=81 rows for any FR before computing per-line metrics. Identity-ratio rows with sub-cent amounts = rounding plugs, ignore. For consumption history, use KBLEW filtered by CURTP - not FMIOI signed sums.

rule: When iterating on a wide table (markdown/excel/report), NEVER remove existing columns when adding new ones. Build the wider table by extending the previous column set in a single canonical script. If a column becomes irrelevant, mark it 'deprecated' but keep displaying it for one session before removing.
why: Session #053: I removed columns 'actual EUR cons USD-eq', 'actual nonEUR USD cons', 'non-EUR breakdown' when adding 'AVC budget USD' + 'AVC available USD'. User caught it twice (the 2nd time with 'PUTA MADRE / me haces perder el tiempo / me reago' — strong frustration signal). This is a costly mistake that breaks user trust.
how_to_apply: When updating a previously-shown table: (1) read the user's last shown column list, (2) the new table MUST contain >= those columns + the additions, (3) if you must restructure, show the column-mapping diff explicitly first and ask. Use a single source-of-truth script that builds incrementally so columns can never silently drop.

rule: Every aggregation column in a report must explicitly state its filter scope in the header AND in any explanatory note. Allowed scopes: 'per-line' (REFBN+RFPOS), 'per-FONDS+FIPEX' (cover-group context), 'per-fund' (FONDS only), 'system-wide'. NEVER label an aggregation column 'total' without specifying 'total of WHAT'.
why: Session #053: I labeled fund+FIPEX consumption as 'pool EUR cons' and the user reasonably assumed it was per-FR consumption ($170K vs €4,400 line). Caused 3 round-trips of clarification. The right column name was 'fund+FIPEX EUR cons (USD-equiv, broader cover-group context)'.
how_to_apply: For any column derived from SUM/COUNT: name it with explicit scope marker. Examples: 'line cons EUR (this REFBN+RFPOS only)', 'pool USD cons (same FONDS+FIPEX, all FRs)'. Never use bare 'total' or 'cons' without scope qualifier.

rule: In data-extraction scripts (RFC, SQL, file I/O) NEVER use 'except: pass' or 'except Exception: pass'. Silent failures cause downstream tables to show 0 / null without explanation, leading to wrong conclusions. ALWAYS at minimum: 'except Exception as e: print(f"ERROR <context>: {e}", file=sys.stderr)'. For RFC calls in particular, log the WHERE clause and parameters that failed.
why: Session #053: my background FMAVCT script used 'except: pass' to swallow per-fund RFC errors. Result: AVC budget column showed 0 for ALL 64 rows (the WHERE clause syntax differed from earlier successful runs). User caught it ('AVC budget 0 doesn't make sense'). I would have spotted it instantly if errors had been printed.
how_to_apply: grep -n 'except.*pass' on every extraction script before running. Replace with explicit error logging. For batch operations across N entities, log ERR + entity ID so you know which entity failed without re-running.

rule: Before computing any metric over a SAP table (FMIOI, FMIFIIT, FMAVCT, BSEG, etc.), sample 3-5 RAW rows for one specific key and inspect them. This catches: (a) sign convention surprises (FMIOI carryforward pairs), (b) duplicate-row patterns (FMAVCT RVERS), (c) rounding-plug rows ($0.01-0.02 ratio=1.0 in FMIOI), (d) trailing-minus number formats ('1234.56-'). Skipping this step caused 3 wrong interpretations in Session #053.
why: Session #053 mistakes: (1) interpreted $0.01 ratio=1.0 rows as cross-currency consumption (they were rounding plugs), (2) computed open balance as orig-consumed (sign convention wrong for carryforwards), (3) double-counted FMAVCT due to RVERS duplicates. All three would have been caught by 5 minutes of raw-row inspection upfront.
how_to_apply: First action when querying a new table or new key: SELECT * FROM <table> WHERE <key>=<sample> ORDER BY ... LIMIT 10. Print rows. Read them. THEN write the aggregation. Total cost: 30 seconds. Total savings: hours of wrong analysis.

rule: When producing a BCM signatory change spec for DBS, ALWAYS include ALL THREE identifiers: Rule number (90000004/90000005), RY OBJID (8-digit), and STEXT (exact group name). Never describe the target group by entity name alone.
why: During INC-000006313 Part 2 cleanup (Session #052, 2026-04-13), DBS almost added Svein OESTTVEIT to IIEP Validation (RY 50010087) instead of UIS Validation (RY 50036801) — the two groups sit next to each other under rule 90000005, both end with the word 'Validation', and differ only by the prefix. Without the RY OBJID in the spec, DBS picked IIEP by mistake. Had the mis-add gone undetected, Svein would have been authorized to approve IIEP payments against the wrong bank carton (compliance breach). The mis-add was caught because the reconciliation check includes an off-entity ghost scan via the carton file.
how_to_apply: Every change spec row must be a 5-tuple: (Action, Rule, RY_OBJID, STEXT, PERNR). Example: 'ADD, 90000005, 50036801, UIS Validation, 10097358'. When writing the spec, present RY OBJID prominently (not only in a footnote). Warn DBS explicitly about lookalike groups that sit next to the target in the OOCU_RESP tree — name at least one specific adjacent trap (e.g. 'do NOT pick IIEP Validation'). After DBS confirms execution, always re-run bcm_signatory_reconciliation_check.py — if the target RY has the expected members AND the adjacent-entity RYs (same rule, different prefix) are unchanged compared to the previous snapshot, the change was applied to the correct group.

rule: At session close, every 'commitment', 'open follow-up', 'what's next', or 'consider building X' mentioned in the retro MUST have a corresponding PMO H-item before the session is closed. If a retro mentions N follow-up items, PMO must show N new/updated entries traceable to that retro.
why: Session #053 generated 16 follow-up items in retro sections §7/§11/§13 (Brain updates, Knowledge graph edges, skill sap_fm_avc_intelligence, ZFIX object promotions, table registrations). Only 3 were formalized (H38/H39/H40). The other 13 quietly disappeared — discovered only at session #054 audit by the user. This is a systemic trap: retros are written with flow-of-thought commitment language but PMO is the enforcement layer. Without a bridge check, velocity-mode sessions erode the knowledge base silently.
how_to_apply: Before marking a session closed, grep the retro for verbs of commitment (should, need to, must, will, consider, propose, promote, register) and for sections titled 'Open follow-ups', 'Brain updates needed', 'PMO updates', 'What's next'. For each unique action noun phrase, verify a matching H-item exists in PMO_BRAIN.md with session tag. If missing, either (a) add the H-item now, or (b) explicitly write 'DECIDED NOT TO TRACK: <reason>' in the retro so the absence is evidence of decision, not omission. When in doubt, formalize — a false-positive H-item can be killed; a false-negative follow-up is irreversible memory loss.

rule: When extending a table, DataFrame, SQLite schema, Excel sheet, or any tabular artifact, NEVER drop existing columns while adding new ones. Use a single canonical script that all queries extend; columns are append-only unless the user explicitly asks to remove one.
why: Session #053 retro §10 identified this as a 'did worse' pattern, observed twice in the same session: while building br_lines_FINAL.xlsx (INC-BUDGETRATE-EQG inventory), the agent added new analytical columns and silently dropped 3-4 existing ones the user had asked for. The user had to re-request and re-validate, which broke trust in the iteration. Root cause: agent was regenerating the whole DataFrame each iteration from a narrower column list instead of augmenting the existing one. Cost: loss of fidelity + wasted user time.
how_to_apply: Maintain a canonical column list variable at the top of the extraction/build script. When adding a new column: append to the list, never replace it. When tempted to simplify: don't — preserve every column the user has ever asked for. If a column is truly obsolete, ask the user explicitly before dropping. In Excel output: pd.concat([existing_df, new_cols], axis=1), not pd.DataFrame(new_data_only). In SQLite: ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN, never DROP TABLE + CREATE TABLE. Verify column count is >= previous before saving.

rule: Before computing aggregates (sum, avg, count, groupby) on an unfamiliar table or data shape, ALWAYS read 3+ raw rows first to verify sign convention, units, scale, and duplicate semantics. Write the aggregation ONLY after the raw sample is understood.
why: Session #053 retro §10: the agent misinterpreted FMIOI sign convention twice — took absolute sums assuming all rows were positive, when in reality FMIOI WRTTP=81 rows for an FR include creation (positive), period-016 year-end reversal (negative), and period-000 carryforward-in (positive). Naive sum returned inflated commitment numbers. Same pattern with FMAVCT duplicates by RVERS/RPMAX. Root cause: the agent wrote the aggregation SQL before reading any raw row. For unfamiliar SAP tables with cluster or time-dependent semantics, this is guaranteed to produce wrong numbers.
how_to_apply: For any new SAP table the agent queries for the first time in a session: (1) SELECT * FROM table LIMIT 5 with all fields visible; (2) inspect sign patterns of amount fields (HSL*, WRBTR, FKBTR) for the same key across rows; (3) check for version/status columns (RVERS, RPMAX, WRTTP, STATUS); (4) document sign convention in a comment before the aggregation SQL. For FMIOI specifically: always filter by GJAHR + WRTTP and trace by BUDAT for carryforward interpretation. For FMAVCT: dedup by RVERS/RPMAX before summing. For cluster tables (BSEG, KBLE, KBLEW): use the appropriate wrapper FM, never raw RFC_READ_TABLE.

rule: Every aggregation column in a report, Excel sheet, or companion MUST explicitly state its filter in the column name or header comment: per-line, per-FR, per-fund, per-FONDS+FIPEX, per-year, per-company-code, per-ledger. Ambiguous 'total' or 'sum' is forbidden.
why: Session #053 retro §10: during the INC-BUDGETRATE-EQG investigation, the agent presented a $170K number as if it were per-FR-line consumption when it was actually per-cover-group pool consumption (FONDS+FIPEX). The user asked 'but that doesn't match the Vonthron case which is one line' — this forced a correction and a re-explanation. The ambiguity was in a column named Total_Consumed which could have meant 4 different things. Unambiguous names at report time prevent misinterpretation at review time.
how_to_apply: Column naming template: <metric>_<filter>_<unit>. Examples: committed_per_FR_USD, available_per_FONDS_FIPEX_USD, actual_per_line_EUR_2026_9H. In Excel headers: freeze row 1 + add a comment explaining the filter. In markdown tables: include filter in the column header, or add a one-line caption below. When in doubt, make the name longer, not shorter. At review: if the user asks 'but is this per X or per Y', the name failed — rename before answering.

evidence_of_violation.incident: $170K pool vs per-line consumption ambiguity, required correction

rule: NEVER execute writes (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/BAPI commits/RFM calls with side effects) against P01 or any production system unless the user EXPLICITLY and unambiguously asks for that specific change on that specific day. 'Unblocked', 'executable', 'ready', 'recommended fix' describe STATE — they are NOT authorization to act. Default mode is evaluation / read-only.
why: Session #055 (2026-04-14): when investigating H48, agent listed H45 as 'unblocked, executable'. User reacted with strong pushback: 'NO actualices NADA en PRD!!! SOlo estas evaluando!!!'. The framing of a task as 'executable' was being read as intent-to-execute. Production writes are high-blast-radius and irreversible — misreading state-language as authorization is a class of defect that must be prevented structurally.
how_to_apply: 1) Any language about production fixes (H45/H46/H48 etc.) is EVALUATION only. 2) Before any RFC call that is not RFC_READ_TABLE, ask 'is this a write?'. If yes, stop and confirm in an explicit question to the user. 3) State writes in the FUTURE CONDITIONAL: 'si ejecutas', 'cuando decidas', never 'ejecuto', 'actualizo'. 4) Presentation of a fix (code diff, SQL INSERT, BAPI call) = design deliverable, never auto-implementation. 5) When user says 'ok proceder', verify they mean execute-write vs continue-investigation — if ambiguous, ask. 6) Applies to: P01 always. D01 if change is destructive or affects shared dev state. Quality_gate: any PR or script that writes to P01 must be approved line-by-line in chat before running.

rule: In any custom ABAP program, `CALL TRANSACTION ... USING <bdc> MODE 'E'` (display on error) opens SAPGUI on every BDC error. When the CALL TRANSACTION sits inside a loop driven by `GET <table>` / `LDB_PROCESS` / large `LOOP AT`, the cumulative per-error GUI handshake consumes work-process time proportional to WAN RTT. On slow field-office paths this breaches `rdisp/max_wprun_time` and the dispatcher aborts the caller's LDB fetch with a TIME_OUT whose stack frame points at the LDB's own SELECT, not at the CALL TRANSACTION. Diagnose by checking (a) `CONSTANTS ... VALUE 'E'` declarations, (b) whether the BDC call site is inside a loop body, (c) whether the dump stack bottoms out in standard SAP LDB code (SAPDBSDF, SAPDBADA, etc.). Fix: MODE 'N' (silent) + `MESSAGES INTO <tab>` + post-loop error reporting in the list output. Canonical instance: YTBAE002.abap:27 + :723/:771/:819/:853 (INC-000006906, Claim 54).
why: Session #058 (2026-04-20): INC-000006906 TIME_OUT dump at SAPDBSDF:1983 PUT_BSIS was misdiagnosed for multiple sessions as (a) YBANK set coverage gap (Claim 52, falsified by source grep), (b) empty-range unbounded LDB scan (Claim 53, falsified by user's non-empty GR_SAKNR_OI), (c) data volume (13 BSIS rows, falsified). The dump hint text 'result set too large' and the LDB-code stack frame misdirected every early hypothesis. The real mechanism was only visible after user reproduced same program, same data, same user from HQ LAN in ~2 seconds vs Maputo WAN TIME_OUT. Three-path field reproduction (VPN / Paris / Maputo) isolated network as the decisive axis. Had this rule existed in the brain at the start of the investigation, the YTBAE002.abap:27 MODE 'E' declaration would have been the first code anchor checked, not the last.
how_to_apply: 1) When triaging any TIME_OUT dump in an LDB callback (PUT_BSIS, PUT_BSAS, PUT_BSAD, PUT_BSIK, PUT_BSAK, PUT_BKPF, PUT_COEP, etc.), search the CALLER source for `MODE 'E'` and `CALL TRANSACTION` in parallel. 2) Flag any custom program with `CONSTANTS: <name> TYPE C VALUE 'E'` + at least one `CALL TRANSACTION ... MODE <that_const>` as NETWORK_COUPLED regardless of whether it has dumped yet. 3) Before recommending any other root cause for a field-office TIME_OUT, ask 'does the program open a GUI dialog on BDC error?'. 4) Fix pattern is always the same: MODE 'N' + `MESSAGES INTO tab` + `PERFORM add_to_accumulator USING tab` at each call site + render accumulator post-loop in the list output.
